content:
# Genesis Chapter 49 — PNEUMA (Dynamic Equivalence) Translation

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 28v–29r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_049_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_049_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0053 (page 49), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0054 (page 50)

---

*The Summons*

**49:1** Jacob called his sons together and said:

"Gather around me, all of you — gather and listen,
sons of Jacob; hear your father Israel.
I will tell you what will come upon you
in the days to come.

**49:2** Assemble and hear, sons of Jacob;
listen to Israel your father.

---

*Reuben — First and Fallen*

**49:3** Reuben, you are my firstborn,
my strength, the first fruit of my vigor —
supreme in rank, supreme in power.

**49:4** But you are turbulent as floodwater —
you will not keep the lead.
For you climbed into your father's bed
and defiled it.
He mounted my couch.

---

*Simeon and Levi — Brothers in Bloodshed*

**49:5** Simeon and Levi are brothers —
their swords are weapons of violence.

**49:6** Let me never enter their council,
let me have no part in their assembly.
In their rage they killed men;
in their willfulness they hamstrung oxen.

**49:7** Cursed be their anger, for it is fierce;
cursed be their wrath, for it is savage.
I will divide them in Jacob
and scatter them throughout Israel.

---

*Judah — Lion, Scepter, and Coming King*

**49:8** Judah — your brothers will praise you;
your hand will grip your enemies by the throat;
your father's sons will bow down before you.

**49:9** Judah is a young lion.
From the kill, my son, you have climbed back up.
He crouches, he stretches out like a lion —
like a lioness — who dares rouse him?

**49:10** The scepter will not depart from Judah,
nor the ruler's staff from between his feet,
until Shiloh comes —
and to him shall be the allegiance of the peoples.

**49:11** He ties his donkey to the vine,
his colt to the choicest branch.
He washes his garments in wine,
his robes in the blood of grapes.

**49:12** His eyes are darker than wine,
his teeth whiter than milk.

---

*Zebulun — Harbor on the Sea*

**49:13** Zebulun will dwell by the seashore
and become a harbor for ships;
his border will reach toward Sidon.

---

*Issachar — The Bone-Weary Beast*

**49:14** Issachar is a strong-boned donkey
lying down between the sheepfolds.

**49:15** He saw that his resting place was good,
and that the land was pleasant —
so he bent his shoulder to the burden
and became a laboring slave.

---

*Dan — The Roadside Serpent*

**49:16** Dan will govern his people
as one of the tribes of Israel.

**49:17** Dan will be a serpent in the road,
a viper along the path,
that bites the horse's heels
so that its rider falls backward.

**49:18** I wait for your salvation, O LORD.

---

*Gad — The Raiding Counter-Raider*

**49:19** Gad — raiders will raid him,
but he will raid at their heels.

---

*Asher — The Rich Table*

**49:20** Asher's food will be rich,
and he will supply royal delicacies.

---

*Naphtali — The Doe Set Free*

**49:21** Naphtali is a doe let loose —
he speaks beautiful words.

---

*Joseph — The Fruitful Vine by the Spring*

**49:22** Joseph is a fruitful vine,
a fruitful vine beside a spring —
his branches climb over the wall.

**49:23** Archers attacked him bitterly,
shooting at him, pressing him hard.

**49:24** But his bow held steady,
his arms stayed nimble —
by the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob,
by the name of the Shepherd, the Rock of Israel,

**49:25** by your father's God, who helps you,
by El Shaddai, who blesses you —
with the blessings of heaven above,
blessings of the deep that crouches beneath,
blessings of the breast and the womb.

**49:26** The blessings of your father
surpass the blessings of the ancient mountains,
the bounty of the everlasting hills.
May they rest on the head of Joseph,
on the brow of the one set apart among his brothers.

---

*Benjamin — The Ravenous Wolf*

**49:27** Benjamin is a ravenous wolf:
in the morning he devours the prey,
in the evening he divides the plunder."

---

*All Twelve Blessed*

**49:28** All these are the twelve tribes of Israel, and this is what their father said to them when he blessed them — blessing each one with the blessing appropriate to him.

---

*Jacob's Burial Command*

**49:29** Then he commanded them, saying: "I am about to be gathered to my people. Bury me with my fathers in the cave in the field of Ephron the Hittite — **49:30** in the cave in the field of Machpelah, near Mamre in the land of Canaan, the field that Abraham bought from Ephron the Hittite as a burial site. **49:31** There they buried Abraham and his wife Sarah. There they buried Isaac and his wife Rebekah. And there I buried Leah. **49:32** The field and the cave in it were purchased from the sons of Heth."

**49:33** When Jacob finished giving instructions to his sons, he drew his feet up onto the bed, breathed his last, and was gathered to his people.

---

## Translation Notes

### vv. 1–2 — "In the Days to Come" (*be'acharit hayyamim*)

**בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים** (*be'acharit hayyamim*, "in the latter days / in the end of days") is a formula charged with prophetic weight. It occurs throughout the Hebrew prophets — Numbers 24:14; Deuteronomy 31:29; Isaiah 2:2; Micah 4:1; Daniel 10:14 — always pointing toward a horizon beyond the immediate future, toward the ultimate end of the story. Jacob is not telling his sons merely what will happen to their tribes in the next generation; he is speaking from a prophetic vantage point about the far end of the line he is inaugurating. "In the days to come" is deliberately more open than "in the near future." The poem that follows is both historical tribal description and something more: a sightline cast across centuries.

The paired verbs of the summons — **הֵאָסְפוּ** (*he'asfu*, "gather yourselves, assemble") in verse 1 and **הִקָּבְצוּ** (*hiqqavtzu*, "gather, collect") in verse 2 — are near synonyms both connoting the collective drawing together of a community. The doubling creates a liturgical solemnity: this is not a casual announcement but a covenantal gathering, comparable to the assembly-scenes of later covenant-renewal ceremonies (Deuteronomy 31:28; Joshua 24:1).

---

### v. 4 — *Pachaz ka-mayim* ("Turbulent as Floodwater")

**פַּחַז כַּמַּיִם** (*pachaz ka-mayim*) — the root **פחז** (*pachaz*) is rare, appearing also in Judges 9:4 (where it describes worthless, reckless men) and Zephaniah 3:4 (where it describes faithless prophets). It connotes unrestrained, undisciplined movement — momentum without self-control. The water image is precise: water follows the path of least resistance, cannot be held once released, floods where it should not go, and wastes itself. Reuben's sin — mounting Bilhah, Jacob's concubine, an act first reported without editorial comment in Genesis 35:22 — is described here in the vocabulary of moral turbulence. He could not hold himself back. He had the mass and momentum of the firstborn and none of the discipline.

**חִלַּלְתָּ** (*khillalta*, "you defiled") is from **חלל** (*chalal*, to pierce, profane, desecrate) — the sacral vocabulary of defilement. Reuben's act is not merely adultery; it is the violation of a sanctified boundary. The plural **מִשְׁכְּבֵי אָבִיךָ** (*mishkevei avikha*, "your father's beds") — note the plural — is likely an abstract plural denoting the entire domain of the marriage-bed, the intimate inner sanctum of the father's household. The violation is described with deliberate avoidance of the subject: "he mounted my couch" — the subject shifts to the third person in the Hebrew, as if Jacob cannot quite say the name.

---

### vv. 5–7 — Simeon and Levi: *Kelei Khamas*, Weapons of Violence

**כְּלֵי חָמָס מְכֵרֹתֵיהֶם** (*klei khamas mekhroteihem*) — "weapons of violence are their *m'kherot*." The word **מְכֵרֹת** (*m'kherot*) is a hapax legomenon; its meaning is genuinely uncertain. Proposals include "swords/knives" (based on Akkadian cognates), "schemes," "kinship," or "wares, stock in trade." The LXX renders it **συνετέλεσαν ἀδικίαν ἐξ αἱρέσεως αὐτῶν** — "they completed injustice from their own choice" — treating the word as pointing to deliberate volition. The rendering "weapons of violence are their stock in trade" reads the phrase as characterizing Simeon and Levi's essential identity: they are defined by their instruments of destruction.

**עִקְּרוּ-שׁוֹר** (*iqqeru-shor*, "they hamstrung an ox/oxen") — the verb **עָקַר** (*aqar*, to hamstring, to disable the tendon of a working animal's leg) describes brutal, gratuitous destruction beyond killing. To hamstring a working animal is not merely to slaughter it; it is to cripple it, to leave it in helpless pain. The reference is to the aftermath of the Shechem massacre of Genesis 34 — though that chapter does not specifically mention hamstringing, the pillaging of the city (34:27–29) included livestock. The singular "ox" (*shor*) may be a collective or may reflect a specific tradition not preserved in the narrative text.

---

### v. 8 — Judah: The Name-Play

**יְהוּדָה אַתָּה יוֹדוּךָ אַחֶיךָ** (*Yehudah attah yodukha akhekha*) — "Judah, you — your brothers will *praise* you." The pun on the name Judah is explicit and unmistakable. **יְהוּדָה** (*Yehudah*) was given by Leah in Genesis 29:35 when she said **הַפַּעַם אוֹדֶה אֶת-יְהוָה** (*happa'am odeh et-YHWH*, "this time I will praise the LORD") — from the root **יָדָה** (*yadah*, to praise, to give thanks, to confess). Now Jacob's oracle opens by activating that etymology: the praised-one (Judah) will be *praised* (*yodukha*) by his brothers. What was a mother's grateful exclamation in the birth-tent becomes, in the old man's dying poem, a tribal destiny. The name carries its meaning forward.

---

### v. 9 — Three Lion-Images

The oracle advances through three distinct lion-terms in quick sequence: **גּוּר אַרְיֵה** (*gur aryeh*, a young lion — specifically a lion that has already made a kill and returned from the hunt), then **אַרְיֵה** (*aryeh*, the full-grown lion lying down in settled power), then **לָבִיא** (*lavi*, the lioness — the most dangerous member of the pride, nursing young, unpredictable). The progression is cub-to-sovereign-to-most-fearsome. "Who dares rouse him?" — **מִי יְקִימֶנּוּ** (*mi yeqimennu*) — the same rhetorical challenge formula appears in the Balaam oracle of Numbers 24:9, explicitly invoking this verse. No answer is expected; no answer is given. The lion lies at ease. No one stirs it.

---

### v. 10 — The Shiloh Crux

**עַד כִּי-יָבֹא שִׁילֹה** (*ad ki-yavo Shiloh*, "until Shiloh comes") — this is the most contested phrase in the entire chapter and one of the most debated in the Hebrew Bible. The word **שִׁילֹה** has been interpreted across three millennia as:

1. **Shiloh as city**: the sanctuary city of Judges–Samuel (Joshua 18:1; 1 Samuel 1). "Until he comes to Shiloh" = until the ark arrives at the cultic center. This is historically plausible but contextually flat — the scepter oracle deserves a more exalted fulfillment.

2. **Shiloh as a composite** (*shay-lo*, "tribute to him" or *shello*, "whose it is"): "until tribute/dominion comes to the one to whom it belongs." This reading is strongly supported by the LXX (see LXX Variants section below) and by Ezekiel 21:32's similar phrase **אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ הַמִּשְׁפָּט** (*asher-lo ha-mishpat*, "to whom the right/judgment belongs"). Many modern scholars prefer this approach.

3. **Shiloh as a messianic title**: "the one to whom it belongs" or "the peaceful one" (*shalom*-related). The Targums (Onkelos, Pseudo-Jonathan, Neofiti) explicitly read this as "Messiah." This is the dominant reading in both rabbinic and patristic tradition.

Per the instruction, this translation renders *Shiloh* literally as the proper noun in the MT — preserving the text's own deliberate ambiguity. A translation should not resolve what the Hebrew leaves open.

**וְלוֹ יִקְּהַת עַמִּים** (*v'lo yiqqehat ammim*, "and to him shall be the allegiance of the peoples") — **יִקְּהַת** (*yiqqehat*) is another hapax legomenon, probably from a root related to **קָוָה** (*qavah*, to hope, to wait, to expect) or possibly **יָקַהּ** (*yaqah*, to obey). The sense is of nations yielding their willing obedience, allegiance, or hope to the coming one. "Allegiance" captures the combination of authority and willing submission; the LXX's **προσδοκία ἐθνῶν** ("expectation of the nations") adds the eschatological note that they are *waiting for* this one.

---

### vv. 11–12 — The Wine Cascade: Hyperbole as Theology

These two verses describe a land so overflowing with abundance that vines are used as hitching posts and wine serves as laundry water. This is deliberate comic hyperbole — the language of eschatological excess. The choicest vine (**שֹׂרֵקָה**, *soreqah*) is so abundant it can serve as a fence. Wine so plentiful it replaces water. **בְּדַם-עֲנָבִים** (*b'dam anavim*, "in the blood of grapes") — this kenning for wine, using **דָּם** (*dam*, blood), appears also in Deuteronomy 32:14. It is a striking phrase: grape-blood, the life-force of the fruit. The verse has generated intensive messianic and eucharistic interpretation in early Christianity precisely because of this phrase — garments washed in blood-red wine, a king who presides over impossible abundance. The imagery recurs in Isaiah 63:1–6, in Joel 3:18, and in the eschatological banquet traditions of the New Testament.

**חַכְלִילִי עֵינַיִם מִיָּיִן** (*khakhlili einayim mi-yayin*) — the color-term **חַכְלִילִי** (*khakhlili*) is rare and uncertain; it suggests deep redness, darkening, lustrous depth. The phrase can be read as "eyes darkened from wine" (the image of a man at ease in his abundance, wine-drowsy) or "eyes more lustrous than wine" (the image of radiant, wine-dark beauty). The parallel with white teeth from milk-richness supports the picture of physical vitality and abundance: this is a people so well-fed, so abundantly blessed, that it shows in their faces.

---

### v. 14 — Issachar: The Settled Servant

**יִשָּׂשׂכָר חֲמֹר גָּרֶם** (*Yissaschar chamor gerem*, "Issachar is a bone/strong donkey") — **גָּרֶם** (*gerem*, "bone, bony, strong-boned") describes a well-built, durable working animal, not a weak one. This is a tribute to Issachar's strength, though the tribute turns ambiguous immediately. The donkey sees **מְנֻחָה כִּי טוֹב** (*menuchah ki tov*, "that rest was good") — an echo of Genesis 1's repeated **כִּי-טוֹב** (*ki-tov*, "it was good"), here applied ironically to the pleasant land that seduces Issachar into trading freedom for comfort. The donkey lies down between the sheepfolds/saddlebags (**מִשְׁפְּתָיִם**, *mishpetayim*) — in the stall, done traveling — and concludes that settling is better than migrating. The result: **וַיְהִי לְמַס-עֹבֵד** (*vayyehi l'mas oved*, "he became a laboring slave / a tribute-working serf"). The pleasant land cost him his freedom. Issachar is not cursed; but the oracle is a clear-eyed assessment of a tribe that chose the comfortable path and paid for it in servitude.

---

### v. 17 — Dan: The *Shefifon*

**שְׁפִיפֹן** (*shefifon*) — this appears nowhere else in the Hebrew Bible: an absolute hapax legomenon. The word is universally understood to denote a venomous serpent, most likely a horned viper (*Cerastes cornutus* or related species) — a small, sand-colored snake that ambushes prey from concealment, difficult to see, strike from below. The LXX Vaticanus renders it oddly as **κέρας ἐπὶ τρίβου** ("a horn on the path") — apparently reading the word's shape differently or guessing at its meaning from context. The Vulgate's *coluber* (serpent) is more reliable. The image is of asymmetric, hidden warfare: Dan is not the lion of open battle; Dan bites at heels, drops riders unexpectedly, and fights from cover. Many interpreters have seen Samson (Judges 13–16, tribe of Dan) in this oracle — the judge who defeated Philistines through surprise and misdirection rather than conventional warfare.

---

### v. 18 — The Interposed Prayer

**לִישׁוּעָתְךָ קִוִּיתִי יְהוָה** (*li-yeshu'atekha qivviti YHWH*, "for your salvation I wait, O LORD") — this single verse is the anomaly of the chapter. It breaks the unbroken run of tribal oracles and inserts a direct prayer in Jacob's first-person voice addressed to God. No other oracle is interrupted this way. Structurally, the verse falls at the midpoint of the poem: six oracles have been given, six remain. Its placement after the Dan oracle, with its serpent-and-ambush imagery, is almost certainly deliberate: Jacob sees in Dan's future something unsettling, something venomous, and he cries out to God before continuing.

**קִוִּיתִי** (*qivviti*) — the verb is **קָוָה** (*qavah*), to wait, to hope with strained expectancy. The same verb appears in Isaiah 40:31 ("those who *wait* for the LORD will renew their strength") and throughout the Psalms of trust and patient endurance. **יְשׁוּעָה** (*yeshuah*, salvation, deliverance) is the root of both *Yehoshua* (Joshua) and *Yeshua* — the Hebrew word for "he saves, he delivers." Jacob's prayer is theologically compact: the man who spent his life scheming and struggling now says only this — *I wait for your rescue, LORD.* It is the prayer of a patriarch who has, at the end of everything, learned to wait.

---

### v. 19 — Gad: Triple Wordplay

**גָּד גְּדוּד יְגוּדֶנּוּ וְהוּא יָגֻד עָקֵב** (*Gad gedud yegudennu vehu yagud aqev*) — one of the most untranslatable lines in Genesis. The name **גָּד** (*Gad*) sounds nearly identical to **גְּדוּד** (*gedud*, a raiding band, a troop) and the verb **גָּדַד** (*gadad*, to raid, slash, attack). The oracle fires three phonetic echoes: Gad / gedud (raiders will raid him) / yagudennu (they will attack him) / yagud (he will raid) / aqev (at their heels). The final word **עָקֵב** (*aqev*, heels) is a deliberate recall of Jacob's own name-etymology (*Ya'akov*, heel-grabber, from Genesis 25:26) and of Genesis 3:15 (the serpent strikes at the heel). Gad is the heel-striker — the counterattacker who turns the tables. The oracle is entirely in sound: Gad's future is built into the reverberations of his own name.

---

### v. 21 — Naphtali: Doe and Beautiful Speech

**נַפְתָּלִי אַיָּלָה שְׁלֻחָה הַנֹּתֵן אִמְרֵי-שָׁפֶר** (*Naphtali ayalah sheluchah ha-noten imrei-shafer*) — "Naphtali is a doe set free who gives beautiful words." The image is of a released animal — the cooped-up doe let loose into open country — swift, graceful, joyfully free. **אִמְרֵי-שָׁפֶר** (*imrei-shafer*, "sayings of grace/beauty") — the root **שָׁפֶר** (*shafer*) denotes elegance, pleasantness, beauty in expression. Naphtali is a tribe of eloquent speech and lyrical swiftness. The oracle fits the later tradition: Barak of the tribe of Naphtali (Judges 4:6) and the great victory song of Deborah and Barak in Judges 5 — *imrei-shafer* in the most literal sense. The LXX diverges significantly here, reading Naphtali as a tree rather than a doe (see LXX Variants below); the MT's doe-and-speech image is more vivid and should be preferred.

---

### v. 22 — Joseph: *Ben Porat*

**בֵּן פֹּרָת יוֹסֵף בֵּן פֹּרָת עֲלֵי-עָיִן** (*ben porat Yosef, ben porat alei-ayin*, "Joseph is a fruitful branch, a fruitful branch beside a spring") — the construction **בֵּן פֹּרָת** (*ben porat*) uses the idiom "son of X" to mean "a being constituted by X." **פֹּרָת** is from **פָּרָה** (*parah*, to be fruitful, to bear fruit) — the same root as **פְּרוּ וּרְבוּ** (*peru ur'vu*, "be fruitful and multiply") of Genesis 1:28. Joseph is the embodiment of fruitfulness. He is placed beside **עָיִן** (*ayin*, a spring, a water source) — the image of Psalm 1:3's tree planted by streams of water, drawing perpetual nourishment. His branches (**בָּנוֹת**, *vanot*, literally "daughters" but here boughs or climbing shoots) **צָעֲדָה עֲלֵי-שׁוּר** (*tsa'adah alei-shur*, "step/climb over the wall") — the fruitfulness overflows its natural container. Nothing can confine Joseph's blessing.

---

### v. 24 — The Mighty One and the Shepherd-Rock

**מִיְּדֵי אֲבִיר יַעֲקֹב** (*mi-yedei avir Ya'akov*, "by the hands of the Mighty One of Jacob") — **אֲבִיר** (*avir*) is an ancient divine epithet related to the bull-root, connoting raw, virile, warrior-strength. The title **אֲבִיר יַעֲקֹב** (*Avir Ya'akov*, "the Mighty One of Jacob") appears in Isaiah 1:24, 49:26, 60:16, and Psalm 132:2,5 — always with the force of a champion who fights for his people, the God who has tied his strength to Jacob's line.

**מִשָּׁם רֹעֶה אֶבֶן יִשְׂרָאֵל** (*misham ro'eh even Yisrael*, "from there the Shepherd, the Rock of Israel") — two divine epithets in apposition. **רֹעֶה** (*ro'eh*, shepherd) — the image of the faithful herder who leads to pasture, seeks the lost, and carries the weak; first invoked in Jacob's blessing of Ephraim and Manasseh in Genesis 48:15 ("the God who has shepherded me all my life"). **אֶבֶן** (*even*, stone, rock) — the immovable foundation, the bedrock that does not shift. God is simultaneously the tender guide and the unshakeable ground. The pairing anticipates Psalm 23 (God as shepherd) and Psalm 18 (God as rock and fortress) — the two images that will become the dominant metaphors for divine protection in Israel's poetry.

---

### v. 25 — El Shaddai and the Threefold Blessing

**אֵל שַׁדַּי** (*El Shaddai*) — preserved untranslated per established convention. This is the covenant name under which God appeared to Abraham (Genesis 17:1) and to Jacob himself (Genesis 35:11). Its reappearance here in the Joseph blessing ties the Joseph oracle directly to the Abrahamic covenant — Joseph receives the blessings that flow from the original patriarchal promise.

The threefold blessing that follows is a cosmological enumeration of everything that can nourish and sustain life:
- **בִּרְכֹת שָׁמַיִם מֵעָל** (*birkot shamayim me'al*, "blessings of heaven above") — rain, dew, sunlight, all that descends from above
- **בִּרְכֹת תְּהוֹם רֹבֶצֶת תָּחַת** (*birkot tehom rovetzet takhat*, "blessings of the deep that crouches beneath") — the underground springs, the aquifers, the subterranean waters that feed wells and rivers; the image of **תְּהוֹם** (*tehom*, the deep) from Genesis 1:2 now domesticated and made to serve fruitfulness
- **בִּרְכֹת שָׁדַיִם וָרָחַם** (*birkot shadayim va-rakham*, "blessings of breast and womb") — bodily fruitfulness, nursing and childbearing, the blessings that make a small family into a great nation

The word **שָׁדַיִם** (*shadayim*, breasts) may carry a secondary resonance with **שַׁדַּי** (*Shadday*) in the very next clause — some scholars have suggested the name El Shaddai may derive from or echo the root of abundance and nourishment. Whether intentional or not, the proximity of *Shadday* and *shadayim* in these verses creates a sonic theology: the God of the name is the God of the blessing.

---

### v. 26 — *Nazir Echav* ("Set Apart Among His Brothers")

**נְזִיר אֶחָיו** (*nezir echav*, "the one set apart / consecrated among his brothers") — **נְזִיר** (*nazir*) is the term that will later designate the Nazirite — the person separated to God by special vow (Numbers 6; cf. Samson in Judges 13:5, Samuel in 1 Samuel 1:11). Before the law, it carries its root meaning: separated, distinguished, crowned apart. Joseph is the *nazir* of his brothers — the consecrated one, the singled-out one.

The irony is devastating and beautiful. Joseph was separated from his brothers by force — thrown into the pit, sold to Egypt, absent for two decades. The separation that looked like abandonment and death was in fact the divine *setting-apart* that would save them all. What his brothers meant as rejection became, in God's economy, consecration. The *nazir* who was stripped of his ornamented coat receives in the end the blessings of mountains and hills and deep and heaven — the most elaborately crowned of all twelve sons.

---

### v. 27 — Benjamin: The Wolf

**בִּנְיָמִין זְאֵב יִטְרָף** (*Binyamin ze'ev yitraf*, "Benjamin is a ravenous wolf") — the tribal oracle of Benjamin is the sharpest, most compressed, most militaristic in the collection. The wolf tears; the wolf hunts at both ends of the day; the wolf divides what it kills. Benjamin is a tribe of warriors: Ehud (Judges 3:15), the left-handed assassin; Saul (1 Samuel 9:1), Israel's first king; Jonathan (1 Samuel 14), the warrior-prince; the tribe that nearly destroyed itself in the civil war of Judges 19–21; and Paul of Tarsus (Philippians 3:5), who describes himself simply as "of the tribe of Benjamin" with no apparent apology for the wolf-image. The oracle is admiring rather than condemnatory — this is a compliment in the language of tribal valor.

---

### vv. 29–33 — The Cave of Machpelah and the Gentle Death

**נֶאֱסַפְתִּי אֶל-עַמִּי** (*ne'esafti el-ammi*, "I am being gathered to my people") — the verb **אָסַף** (*asaf*, to gather, collect) describes death as an act of reception rather than extinction. The community of the dead ancestors receives the dying patriarch — he is *gathered in* to them. The phrase appears also for Abraham's death (Genesis 25:8) and Isaac's (Genesis 35:29), and later for Aaron (Numbers 20:24) and Moses (Numbers 27:13; Deuteronomy 32:50). It is distinct from burial (recorded separately) and from the cessation of breath (also recorded separately) — it points toward something that mere physical death does not exhaust.

Jacob's burial command specifies the cave of Machpelah three times with accumulating detail (vv. 29–32), the insistence of a man for whom this matters enormously. He is not to be buried in Egypt — however gracious Pharaoh's hospitality has been. His body belongs to Canaan, to the plot of land Abraham purchased as the first permanent Israelite holding in the land of promise. The triple specification is almost architectural: cave in Ephron's field / cave in Machpelah / near Mamre in Canaan. He is mapping the way home.

**וַיְכַל יַעֲקֹב לְצַוֹּת** (*vaykhal Ya'akov l'tzavot*, "and Jacob finished commanding/giving instructions") — the verb **כָּלָה** (*kalah*, to finish, complete, bring to an end) — Jacob completes his work. Then: **וַיֶּאֱסֹף רַגְלָיו אֶל-הַמִּטָּה** (*vayyeesof raglav el-ha-mittah*, "he gathered his feet onto the bed") — the final physical act: drawing the legs up from the bedside into the bed, the body collecting itself. **וַיִּגְוַע** (*vayyigva'*, "and he expired/breathed his last") — the quiet verb for the final breath, used for Abraham (Genesis 25:8) and Isaac (Genesis 35:29). And then: **וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו** (*vayyei'asef el-ammav*, "and he was gathered to his people"). Jacob dies in three words: feet gathered, breath given out, self received. There is no agony in the text. There is only completion.

---

## LXX Variants (Codex Vaticanus, Pages 49–50)

### v. 1 — "In the Last Days"

The MT's **בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים** (*be'acharit hayyamim*) is rendered in the Vaticanus LXX as **ἐπ' ἐσχάτων τῶν ἡμερῶν** (*ep' eschatōn tōn hēmerōn*, "at the last of the days") — using **ἔσχατος** (*eschatos*, last, final, extreme), the root of the New Testament's **ἐσχατολογία** (eschatology). The LXX preserves and arguably enhances the eschatological force of the phrase.

### v. 4 — Reuben's Defilement

For **חִלַּלְתָּ** (*khillalta*, "you defiled"), the LXX reads **ἐβεβήλωσας** (*ebebēlōsas*, "you profaned, desecrated") — from **βεβηλόω** (*bebēloō*), to render profane, to desecrate the sacred. This is a strong word used in the LXX for the profanation of holy things (Leviticus 20:3; Ezekiel 22:8, 26). The choice emphasizes the sacrilegious rather than merely sexual character of Reuben's act — he violated something consecrated.

For the MT's plural **מִשְׁכְּבֵי אָבִיךָ** (*mishkevei avikha*, "your father's beds"), the LXX reads **τὴν κοίτην τοῦ πατρός σου** (*tēn koitēn tou patros sou*, "the bed of your father") — singular. The MT plural is likely an abstract plural; the LXX specifies the act more concretely.

### v. 9 — Three Lion-Terms

The MT uses three distinct Hebrew lion-terms: **גּוּר אַרְיֵה** (*gur aryeh*, lion cub), **אַרְיֵה** (*aryeh*, adult lion), **לָבִיא** (*lavi*, lioness). The LXX Vaticanus matches all three precisely with Greek equivalents: **σκύμνος λέοντος** (*skymnos leontos*, "cub of a lion"), **λέων** (*leōn*, lion), **λέαινα** (*leaina*, lioness). This is one of the most careful translation decisions in the chapter — the Vaticanus translator clearly recognized the three-stage escalation and reproduced it accurately.

### v. 10 — The Shiloh Verse: The Most Significant Variant

This is the defining LXX divergence in Genesis 49. Where the MT reads **עַד כִּי-יָבֹא שִׁילֹה** (*ad ki-yavo Shiloh*, "until Shiloh comes"), the Vaticanus text reads:

**ἕως ἂν ἔλθῃ τὰ ἀποκείμενα αὐτῷ** (*heōs an elthēi ta apokeimena autōi*)

"Until the things laid up / reserved for him arrive."

The Greek **ἀποκείμενα** (*apokeimena*, the things stored away, reserved, laid up) is the perfect passive participle of **ἀπόκειμαι** (*apokeimai*, to be put away, stored, reserved) — the same word used in 2 Timothy 4:8 ("there is *reserved* for me the crown of righteousness") and Colossians 1:5 ("the hope *laid up* for you in heaven"). The LXX has treated the Hebrew *Shiloh* not as a proper noun or city name but as pointing to a *stored destiny* — an inheritance that has been set aside and is waiting to arrive at the right moment. The nations yield their obedience to the one for whom this reserved inheritance is coming.

How did the LXX reach this reading? Several possibilities: (a) the translator worked from a Hebrew Vorlage reading **שֶׁלֹּה** (*shello*, "that which belongs to him") rather than **שִׁילֹה** (*Shiloh*); (b) the translator interpreted *Shiloh* through the context and rendered its implied sense; (c) the translator read it as cognate to **שַׁי** (*shai*, tribute/gift) + **לוֹ** (*lo*, to him). Whatever the route, the effect is to emphasize that the coming figure has a *reserved inheritance* — not merely a territorial claim or a personal name, but a destiny stored up for him in the divine economy. The nations' allegiance flows toward one for whom something cosmic has been set aside.

The second line, **וְלוֹ יִקְּהַת עַמִּים** (*v'lo yiqqehat ammim*, "and to him shall be the allegiance of the peoples"), is rendered in Vaticanus as **καὶ αὐτὸς προσδοκία ἐθνῶν** (*kai autos prosdokia ethnōn*, "and he himself is the expectation/hope of the nations"). The Greek **προσδοκία** (*prosdokia*, expectation, anticipation, waiting) shifts the emphasis from the nations' *yielding* to the nations' *waiting* — not submission under compulsion but hopeful expectation. This reading became enormously influential in early Christian interpretation: "he himself is the hope the nations are waiting for." The verse in this form was cited as messianic prophecy by Justin Martyr, Irenaeus, and others.

### v. 11 — "Blood of the Grape"

The LXX renders **בְּדַם-עֲנָבִים** (*b'dam anavim*, "in the blood of grapes") as **ἐν αἵματι σταφυλῆς** (*en haimati staphulēs*, "in the blood of the grape-cluster"). The Greek **αἷμα** (*haima*, blood) straightforwardly translates the Hebrew **דָּם** (*dam*), but the choice of *haima* in a passage about washing garments in wine proved significant for early Christian readers who placed this verse in a messianic-eucharistic framework. The LXX here simply follows the MT; the interpretive use of the verse is a later development.

### v. 21 — Naphtali: Doe or Tree?

This is one of the sharpest MT/LXX divergences in the chapter. The MT reads:
**נַפְתָּלִי אַיָּלָה שְׁלֻחָה הַנֹּתֵן אִמְרֵי-שָׁפֶר**
"Naphtali is a doe let loose / who speaks beautiful words."

The Vaticanus LXX reads:
**Νεφθαλὶ στέλεχος ἀνειμένον ἐπιδιδοὺς τῷ καρπῷ κάλλος**
"Naphtali is a spreading trunk/stock, yielding beauty of fruit."

The Greek **στέλεχος** (*stelechos*) is a tree-trunk or plant-stock. The LXX translator appears to have read **אַיָּלָה** (*ayalah*, doe) as **אֵלָה** (*elah*, terebinth/oak tree) — the consonantal difference is minimal (aleph-yod-lamed-heh vs. aleph-lamed-heh) and could reflect either a different Hebrew Vorlage or a scribal/translational confusion. The result is a completely different image: instead of the fleet doe who speaks beautifully, the LXX has a spreading tree that yields beautiful fruit — more agricultural, more static, but still positive.

The MT's doe-and-speech image is more vivid, more consistent with the chapter's use of animal imagery throughout, and more specific to Naphtali's later tradition (the swift, lyrical Deborah-Barak tradition). The MT is to be preferred; the LXX divergence is noted but does not affect the translation.

### v. 22 — Joseph as Growing Son

For **בֵּן פֹּרָת** (*ben porat*, "fruitful branch / son of fruitfulness"), the Vaticanus LXX reads **υἱὸς ηὐξημένος** (*huios ēuxēmenos*, "a son who has grown/increased") — from **αὐξάνω** (*auxanō*, to grow, to increase). This is the same verb used in the LXX for the creation-blessing of Genesis 1:22 (**αὐξάνεσθε καὶ πληθύνεσθε**, "increase and multiply") and the Abrahamic growth-promises. The LXX translator is explicitly connecting Joseph's fruitfulness to the divine mandate of increase that runs through Genesis from creation onward. The MT's vine/branch imagery is lost in the LXX's rendering, which emphasizes growth in a more general sense.

### v. 24 — The Divine Name Cascade

The Vaticanus text handles the four divine titles of vv. 24–25 with varying fidelity to the MT:

**אֲבִיר יַעֲקֹב** (*Avir Ya'akov*, "Mighty One of Jacob"): rendered **ὑπὸ χειρὸς δυνάστου Ιακωβ** (*hupo cheiros dynastou Iakōb*, "by the hand of the mighty ruler of Jacob") — **δυνάστης** (*dynastēs*, dynast, powerful ruler) captures political dominion but loses the primal bull-strength of *avir*.

**רֹעֶה אֶבֶן יִשְׂרָאֵל** (*ro'eh even Yisrael*, "Shepherd, Rock of Israel"): rendered **ἐκεῖθεν ὁ κατισχύσας Ισραηλ** (*ekeithen ho katischysas Israēl*, "from there the one who strengthened Israel") — both the shepherd metaphor and the rock metaphor disappear entirely. The LXX appears to have read a different text or to have paraphrased freely. The loss of the dual shepherd-rock image is significant.

**אֵל שַׁדַּי** (*El Shaddai*): rendered **ὑπὸ θεοῦ τοῦ πατρός σου** ("by the God of your father") — losing the *Shaddai* title and rendering it relationally. This is the most significant compression in the passage: El Shaddai's distinctiveness as a divine name is flattened into a generic relational phrase.

Overall, the LXX's handling of the Joseph oracle's divine name cascade is considerably less theologically precise than the MT. The MT preserves four distinct divine epithets in staccato sequence; the LXX collapses them into paraphrase. The Vaticanus text here is notably inferior to the MT for theological study.

### v. 26 — *Nazir* as "Sanctified One"

For **נְזִיר אֶחָיו** (*nazir echav*, "one set apart among his brothers"), Vaticanus reads **ὁ ἡγιασμένος ἐν ἀδελφοῖς** (*ho hēgiasmenos en adelphois*, "the one who has been sanctified among brothers"). The Greek **ἡγιασμένος** (*hēgiasmenos*) is the perfect passive of **ἁγιάζω** (*hagiazō*, to sanctify, make holy, set apart to God) — the same word used throughout the LXX for consecrating priests, dedicating holy objects, and in the New Testament for the sanctification of believers. The LXX makes explicit the *divine consecration* that the MT implies through the Nazirite terminology. Joseph is not merely the distinguished one; he is the *sanctified* one — holy, set apart to God, consecrated by the one who uses human treachery as an instrument of divine calling.

### v. 33 — Jacob's Death: Sabbath Rest

The LXX renders **וַיְכַל יַעֲקֹב לְצַוֹּת** (*vaykhal Ya'akov l'tzavot*, "and Jacob finished giving instructions") as **καὶ κατέπαυσεν Ιακωβ** (*kai katepausen Iakōb*, "and Jacob ceased / rested") — using **καταπαύω** (*katapauō*, to rest, bring to rest, cause to cease). This is the exact verb used in the LXX for God's rest on the seventh day: **κατέπαυσεν ὁ θεός** (*katepausen ho theos*, "God rested," Genesis 2:2). Whether the LXX translator intended the intertextual echo or not, the effect is profound: Jacob's death is described in the language of Sabbath. He rested. His work was complete. The patriarch who was given the name Israel after a night of wrestling came to his rest, as the God who created in six days came to his rest. The MT does not carry this resonance. The LXX reading is an interpretive gift embedded in translation.

---

## Structural Note: The Architecture of the Poem

Genesis 49 is the longest sustained poem in Genesis and, by most scholarly assessments, one of the oldest pieces of Hebrew poetry in the Hebrew Bible. Its arrangement follows a pattern shaped not by birth order but by theological weight:

**Opening Frame (vv. 1–2):** Prophetic summons. All twelve called to hear what will come at the end of days. The gathering is liturgical in character — the patriarch becomes a prophet.

**The Three Displaced Heirs (vv. 3–7):** Reuben (lost preeminence through violation), Simeon and Levi (cursed and scattered for violence). Three eldest sons, three negative oracles. The path to Judah is cleared by the failure of those who had prior claim.

**First Pillar — Judah (vv. 8–12):** The longest oracle. Lion imagery (cub, sovereign, lioness), the immovable scepter, the Shiloh promise, the wine-cascade of impossible abundance. Judah is fourth by birth; he is first by destiny. The poem's first half crests and peaks here.

**Six Middle Tribes (vv. 13–21):** Zebulun (sea-harbor), Issachar (strong donkey, comfortable slave), Dan (roadside viper), Gad (counter-raider), Asher (rich food), Naphtali (eloquent doe). Six brief oracles of varying character. At the precise midpoint — between Dan and Gad — Jacob's personal prayer interrupts everything (v. 18). That prayer is the structural center of the entire poem: twelve tribes, and at the pivot, Jacob cries out to God.

**Second Pillar — Joseph (vv. 22–26):** The second longest oracle. Fruitful vine, the embattled archer, four divine names, the comprehensive blessing from every zone of creation, the brow of the consecrated one. Joseph receives what Reuben lost: the double portion, the preeminence of blessing. The oracle is a theological argument: what was stripped away by hatred was restored by God.

**Closing Coda — Benjamin (v. 27):** One verse. A wolf, morning prey, evening plunder. The youngest son closes the poem with a single compressed image of predatory energy — not elevated like Judah and Joseph, not cursed like Simeon and Levi, but simply the wolf. The poem ends in controlled ferocity.

**Closing Frame (vv. 28–33):** The narrator's summary, Jacob's burial insistence (three specifications of the Machpelah cave), and the death report. The poem's exaltation gives way to prose; the prophet becomes a dying man drawing up his feet.

The poem's deepest theological logic is the consistent reversal of natural expectations. Primogeniture is not destiny. Violence cannot secure a future. The scepter goes to the fourth son, the double portion to the eleventh. What determines destiny is character aligned with God's purpose — Judah who offered himself as surety, Joseph who forgave what should not have been forgivable. The poem knows what history will confirm: from Judah will come the royal line of David, the psalms, the messianic hope; from Joseph (through Ephraim and Manasseh) will come the northern kingdom and its territorial heartland. The two pillars of the poem are the two pillars of the Israelite future.

And at the exact center of it all: *I wait for your salvation, O LORD.* The twelve tribes, the scepter, the fruitful vine — all of it held together by a dying man's prayer of patient trust in the God who is Mighty One, Shepherd, Rock, and El Shaddai.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 28v–29r, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 28v (GEN_049_page_001.jpg) carries Genesis 49:1 through approximately verse 18, encompassing the summons, the oracles of Reuben, Simeon-Levi, Judah, Zebulun, Issachar, Dan, and the interposed prayer of verse 18. Folio 29r (GEN_049_page_002.jpg) carries the remainder — the oracles of Gad, Asher, Naphtali, Joseph, and Benjamin; the closing summary (v. 28); Jacob's burial instructions (vv. 29–32); and his death (v. 33). Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages pa_0049–pa_0050; accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0053 and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0054. The Vaticanus page pa_0049 carries the chapter marker CAP III in the upper left margin, reflecting the LXX section-division system. The most significant Vaticanus variant in this chapter is the Shiloh crux (v. 10): ἕως ἂν ἔλθῃ τὰ ἀποκείμενα αὐτῷ, "until what is stored up for him comes" — a rendering that shaped the entire messianic reading history of this verse in both Jewish and Christian interpretation.*
